display_title: 1989-05_06 — What the Heck Do the Centennial Games Zones Mean?
body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
May-June 1989

What the Heck Do the Centennial Games Zones Mean?

Nothing. In orienteering, the zones mean absolutely nothing. Other sports in the Centennial Games have zone playoffs, similar to semi-finals, at which participants for the final competition in Spokane are chosen. But because orienteering is a demonstration sport, we will not have zone playoffs. However, we are still required to have zone representatives, whose job it is to keep informed about qualification procedures and to choose those people who will complete the orienteering contingent at the Centennial Games.

At the qualifying meets, each of the 6 Centennial Games categories will be assigned to a particular course. To qualify in a specific category, simply sign up for the appropriate course, and inform the people at registration that you want to try to qualify for the Centennial Games. The top 2 people in each category at each qualifying meet automatically qualify for the Centennial Games. The rest of the official competitors will be chosen by a committee consisting of the 8 zone representatives, the meet director, and the state chairman.

General Information

  1. Age Requirement - each participant must be at least 13 years old as of August 10, 1989.
  2. Residency Requirement - each participant must have been a resident of the state of Washington for at least one year prior to the start of the Games. This includes full-time students attending prep schools or colleges in the state, Washington residents who are students outside of the state, and members (and their dependents) of the U.S. Armed Forces residing in Washington who have been posted to a location within Washington for a minimum of one year.
  3. Membership Requirement - each participant must be a member of an orienteering club in the state of Washington.
  4. Entry Fee for the Centennial Games final - $10.00.
  5. Categories
    Junior Female (13-20 years old)
    Junior Male (13-20 years old)
    Open Female
    Open Male
    Masters Female (35 and older)
    Masters Male (35 and older)

Cross-Country Navigation Talks at REI

Just in time for the May 13-14 ROGAINE, former Australian national ROGAINE champion Neil Phillips will give talks on cross-country navigation on May 11 and 12. On Thursday, May 11, he will be at the Bellevue REI at 7:00 p.m. and on Friday, May 12, at the Federal Way REI at 6:00 p.m. Both talks will cost $10, payable at the door. The registration fee includes a copy of a U.S.G.S. map to practice on.

Your Next O’ Trip Could Help WSOA

You can help WSOA (and COC) by making your travel plans to attend upcoming orienteering meets (USOF Convention & “A” meet in Colorado, Centennial Games in Spokane, Western States Championships on Orcas Island, U.S. Championships in California) through Mutual Travel. As our official travel agency, Mutual Travel is sponsoring WSOA by donating a percentage of their commission on travel arrangements made through them for these meets. You will always receive the lowest airfare possible and may be eligible for further discounts in some circumstances.

If you are planning to attend the USOF Convention or associated “A” meet this summer, consider using Mutual. They have arranged an additional discount on Continental Airlines flights for this event (5% off the lowest applicable fare). If you really want to help WSOA, you can choose to pay the lowest applicable fare and donate the difference in fares (5%) to WSOA. Ask the agent for details.

Call Mutual Travel’s group department to make travel arrangements. Tell them you are part of the Washington State Orienteering Association. In Seattle, call 340-6800. Elsewhere in Washington, call toll-free 800-348-8800 (ask for the group department). Nationwide, call 800-426-8284.

Learn Course Design May 22

Come to the COC club meeting on Monday, May 22 and hear Mike Schuh explain how to design and set orienteering courses. The meeting will be held at the University Branch Library at 50th and Roosevelt in Seattle from 7-8:30 p.m..

Bus To U.S. Championships

If you plan to attend the U.S. Championships near San Francisco the first weekend of October, and are interested in chartering a bus, call Dave Tallent at 392-3061.

Woodland Park Relay

Those people who plan to attend the May 21 relay at Woodland Park, and who already have a 2-person team, please call Mike Schuh (783-3960) to pre-register. Although pre-registration is not required, it would be greatly appreciated because it makes planning much easier.

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we’ll start with positive roster changes as of 24 April 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Elizabeth Anderson, Dave Berliner, Dina Boon, Lee Daneker, Jed Dixon, Glenn Gordon, Bob & Suzy Lynch, Stewart McClellan, Gloria & Tom Schuh, Judy Shannon, Roger Stocker, Walt & Nancy Turley, Bill Yost family. If you all don't come to meets, you’ll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as a regular meet, meeting, or pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade spnonsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs’ functions. Now on to the fun stuff. Ross Stevens and his wife are proud parents of a new baby, Heather, born 2 April; perhaps she will be ready for PNWOF string O’ in August 1990....Long-time Cascade member and current newsletter editor, Debbie Newell, not only graduated from the U.W. with a degree in Scientific and Technical Writing, but quickly found a full-time job. She is working for PEI Consultants, Inc., where she is already bringing her co-workers to O meets. One whom she dragged out is Birgitta Farris, originally from Sweden, who left in 1967 and hadn't done any Orienteering since school there; yes, orienteering is a required part of the curriculum in Sweden....It is probably time to re-institute a C.O.C. tradition that was in effect the 1st 5 years that I was a club member - the newsletter mailing get-together. After the newsletter is pasted up and ready to be copied, a group of about 6-11 folks gather round the copy machine. The freshly copied pages will be collated, folded, stapled, labeled, and stamped by a chattering crew that doesn't even notice that they are accomplishing useful work. It was always lots of fun. If anyone is interested in getting together to do the next newsletter, contact the editor, Debbie....Enthusiastic member Thurston Miller will soon graduate from the U. W. library school and move to Newport (in June). He has been bringing lots of his colleagues to meets and has convinced 3 or 4 to join Cascade. We need 10 more like him....If you want more news about people in the club, you’ll have to write something or be sure to tell me something that is for this column.

For the latest meet information, call the ORIENTEERING HOTLINE at 783-3866

Event Key
A - major event on good quality color map, 5 courses minimum
B - major local event, 3-4 courses minimum
C - local club event, 3 or 4 courses
S - special event (bicycle, canoe, ski, night, etc.)
Q - Centennial Games qualifier (everyone welcome)




Club Key
COC - Cascade O Club; EOC - Ellensburg O Club; EWOC - Eastern Washington O Club; NISQ - Nisqually Orienteers; SAMM - Sammamish O Club; WSOA - Washington State O Assoc. BAOC - Bay Area O Club; CVO - Cowichan Valley Orienteers; FWOC - Foothills Wanderers; GVOC - Greater Vancouver O Club; SOCK - Sage O Club of Kamloops; VO - VictOrienteers

Changes from last issue:

  • May 20 Sammamish O Club meet will be special benefit meet for ALS at Union Bay - call Dave Enger (822-6254) for more information
  • June 10 meet at Pt. Defiance changed to June 11 and is no longer a relay

“Bearing 315” is a bi-monthly publication of the Cascade Orienteering Club. You are encouraged to submit orienteering-related material for publication. She is working for PEI Consultants, Inc., where she is already bringing her co-workers to O meets. One whom she dragged out is Birgitta Farris, originally from Sweden, who left in 1967 and hadn't done any Orienteering since school there; yes, orienteering is a required part of the curriculum in Sweden....It is probably time to re-institute a C.O.C. tradition that was in effect the 1st 5 years that I was a club member - the newsletter mailing get-together. After the newsletter is pasted up and ready to be copied, a group of about 6-11 folks gather round the copy machine. The freshly copied pages will be collated, folded, stapled, labeled, and stamped by a chattering crew that doesn't even notice that they are accomplishing useful work. It was always lots of fun. If anyone is interested in getting together to do the next newsletter, contact the editor, Debbie....Enthusiastic member Thurston Miller will soon graduate from the U. W. library school and move to Newport (in June). He has been bringing lots of his colleagues to meets and has convinced 3 or 4 to join Cascade. We need 10 more like him....If you want more news about people in the club, you’ll have to write something or be sure to tell me something that is for this column.
